metadata.title: Brand Foundation: Mission, Purpose, Vision, Values + a UVP You'd Say Out Loud
metadata.playlist: Name It & Brand It — Your Strategic Identity
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 6 brief — Brand Strategy + Naming

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 1 of 7. Before you name the tool, you need to know what it stands for. Four short statements — Mission, Purpose, Vision, and Values — decide everything downstream: the name you pick, the portfolio you build, the way you describe it in an interview. This episode builds those four and adds one more: a UVP, a Unique Value Proposition, that you could say out loud in a job interview without cringing. One note: if you come from the Conductor Brief, Episode 5 of the 5B series is your starting point — the one-thing field and the conductor note already contain the seeds of the UVP. Pull from there. Let's go.

props.segment: Brand Foundation: Mission, Purpose, Vision, Values + UVP
props.command: Build the 4 foundation statements + a UVP you'd say out loud. Pull seeds from the Conductor Brief's one-thing field.
props.runningText: Mission · Purpose · Vision · Values → UVP: three to five iterations, then the gut-check read-aloud test…
output: Mission: what you do, who you serve, how you create value
Purpose: why — the deeper motivation beyond money
Vision: where — the ultimate impact you're working toward
Values: 3–5 principles you actually operate by

beats.narration_text: The four foundation statements. Mission: what you do, who you serve, and how you create value. This is a single sentence. For a personal brand: 'I help hiring managers at data-driven companies make better decisions through machine learning pipelines built on transparent, auditable AI.' For a startup: 'We help marketing analysts identify signal in unstructured news data through AI-powered digest tools that reduce daily review time.' Notice both sentences answer the same three questions: what, who, how. Purpose: why — the motivation deeper than money or the assignment. 'Because most AI systems produce output no one can explain, and unexplainable AI isn't trustworthy.' Purpose is the reason you keep going when the pipeline breaks at midnight. Vision: where — the long-horizon outcome. 'A world where every analyst who uses an AI tool can trace every recommendation back to a source they verified.' Vision can be aspirational and large; it does not have to be achievable this semester. Values: the three to five principles you actually operate by, not aspirational words. 'Precision before speed. Transparency at every gate. Conductor first, automation second.' If you have ten values, you have none. Pick three to five and write one sentence explaining each.

artifactLines: Mission: what · who · how — one sentence. (I/We help [who] achieve [what] through [how].)
Purpose: why — the motivation deeper than the assignment. One sentence.
Vision: where — the long-horizon outcome. Aspirational is fine.
Values: 3–5 you actually operate by. One explanatory sentence each.
If you have 10 values, you have none. Cut to the ones you'd defend.
props.sparkLine: What · Why · Where · How you operate. One sentence each.

beats.act: the uvp formula — three to five iterations
beats.narration_text: The UVP — Unique Value Proposition — is the one statement that says what you or your tool does and why someone should care. The formula has two versions. Personal brand: 'I help [employers/clients] achieve [specific result] through [the unique approach, skills, or method that distinguishes you].' Startup brand: 'Our product helps [target audience] achieve [specific result] through [the distinct offering].' The brackets are not suggestions — fill them with specific words. Not 'data professionals' — 'marketing analysts at mid-size companies who review unstructured news daily.' Not 'better decisions' — 'forty percent shorter daily review time with a documented audit trail.' Write three to five iterations before you decide. First draft: get the structure right. Second draft: replace generic words with specific ones. Third draft: cut every word that doesn't add meaning. Fourth draft: read it out loud and listen for where it sounds like a corporate template. Fifth draft, if needed: fix what sounded wrong. The goal is a sentence you would actually say to a hiring manager, a VC, or your professor — and they would immediately understand what is different about you or your tool.

artifactLines: Personal: 'I help [employers] achieve [specific result] through [unique skills/method].'
Startup: 'Our product helps [audience] achieve [specific result] through [distinct offering].'
Draft 1: get the structure. Draft 2: replace generic nouns with specific ones.
Draft 3: cut words that don't add meaning. Draft 4: read aloud — fix what sounds like a template.
Conductor Brief seed: pull the one_thing field → UVP first draft.
props.sparkLine: 3–5 iterations. Specific beats polished. Template = not done.

beats.narration_text: The gut-check test. When your final UVP is written, read it out loud to someone who has not seen it — a roommate, a classmate, a family member. Ask them one question: 'What does that mean?' If they say 'sounds like every LinkedIn profile,' it is not done. If they say 'oh, so your tool catches the AI's mistakes before they reach the user,' it is working. The signs that a UVP is not done: words like 'leveraging,' 'synergizing,' 'driving value,' 'innovative solutions,' 'cutting-edge.' These are filler. Replace them with the actual noun. Not 'innovative AI solution' — 'a tool that stops a report from being sent when the AI confidence score is below 70.' Another test: could this statement apply to someone else's tool or profile, with just the names changed? If yes, it is not specific enough. The specificity is the value proposition. A UVP that only fits you or your tool is worth something. A UVP that could fit twenty tools is worth nothing.

props.artifactHeading: Would you say this in an interview without cringing?
artifactLines: Read aloud to someone who hasn't seen it — ask: 'What does that mean?'
Red flags: 'leveraging,' 'synergizing,' 'innovative solutions,' 'cutting-edge' — filler. Replace with the actual noun.
Test: could this apply to a different tool with the names changed? If yes — not specific enough.
A UVP that only fits you/your tool has value. A UVP that fits twenty tools has none.
Conductor Brief check: does your UVP match the one_thing and conductor_note?
props.sparkLine: Specific enough to only fit you. That's the goal.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Brand Foundation. Four statements: Mission — what you do, who you serve, how you create value, one sentence. Purpose — why, the motivation deeper than the assignment, one sentence. Vision — the long-horizon outcome. Values — three to five you actually operate by, one explanatory sentence each. Then the UVP: write three to five iterations using the formula, replace generic words with specific ones, read it out loud, and pass the gut-check test — does it only fit you or your tool, or could it apply to twenty others? The Conductor Brief already has the seeds: the one_thing field is the differentiation, the conductor_note is the tone. Pull from there before you open AI. Generic brand work is the top pitfall. Specific-to-you beats polished-but-empty every time. Episode 2 is positioning and pillars.

props.artifactTitle: Brand Foundation: Mission, Purpose, Vision, Values + UVP
artifactLines: 4 statements: Mission (what/who/how) · Purpose (why) · Vision (where) · Values (3–5, one sentence each).
UVP: 3–5 iterations → specific nouns → read aloud → gut-check → only fits you.
Conductor Brief seed: one_thing → differentiation; conductor_note → tone.
Generic = top pitfall. Specific-to-you beats polished-but-empty.
Could it apply to 20 tools? If yes — not done.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Here is the prompt that drafts the foundation from your Conductor Brief. Paste this into Claude and fill in the brackets. The prompt reads: I am writing Part 1A of INFO 7375 Assignment 6 — a brand foundation for [personal brand / my Madison tool called (tool name)]. Here is what I already know: the tool_summary from my brand_config.json is: [paste it]. The one_thing field is: [paste it]. The primary_user is: [paste it]. The conductor_note is: [paste it]. From this information, draft: a Mission statement (one sentence: what / who / how), a Purpose statement (one sentence: the deeper why), a Vision statement (one aspirational sentence), three to five Values with one explanatory sentence each, and five iterations of a UVP using the formula 'I help [who] achieve [what] through [how]' — each iteration more specific than the last. For the UVP iterations, replace generic words like 'innovative' or 'leveraging' with the actual nouns from the tool_summary. After Claude gives you the drafts, apply the gut-check test: read each UVP iteration out loud, and mark the one that would not make you cringe in a job interview. That is your first draft — your job is to rewrite it in your own voice before it goes in the PDF.

props.segment: Brand Foundation: Mission, Purpose, Vision, Values + UVP
props.command: tool_summary: [paste]. one_thing: [paste]. primary_user: [paste]. conductor_note: [paste]. Draft: Mission · Purpose · Vision · Values (3–5) · 5 UVP iterations (each more specific — no 'leveraging' or 'innovative,' use the actual nouns).
props.runningText: after Claude drafts — apply gut-check: read each UVP aloud, pick the one you wouldn't cringe at, then rewrite in your own voice…
